key: General|Python|Tooling|Run a linter suppress by name with reason
claim: Run pylint (or equivalent) on all code; suppress false positives with the warning's symbolic name on the offending line plus a reason, so suppressions stay searchable and revisitable.

Import packages and modules, not individual classes or functions — 'from sound.effects import echo' then echo.EchoFilter(...) keeps every identifier's origin visible (typing/collections.abc are the sanctioned exemptions).

key: General|Python|Imports|Rename only for conflicts or convention
claim: Use 'import y as z' only for standard abbreviations (numpy as np) and 'from x import y as z' only for real conflicts or unwieldy names — gratuitous renames hide origins.

key: General|Python|Imports|Full package paths no relative imports
claim: Import every module by its full package path and avoid relative imports — full paths prevent double-imports and don't depend on what happens to be on sys.path; never assume the main binary's directory is importable.
Raise built-in exception types where they fit — ValueError for a violated argument precondition — rather than inventing a custom class per check.

Never use assert to validate arguments or carry application logic — asserts can be compiled away; the litmus test is that removing the assert must not break the code (pytest test bodies are the exception).

key: General|Python|Exceptions|Custom exceptions inherit and end in Error
claim: Library exceptions inherit from an existing exception class, end in 'Error', and avoid name repetition — foo.Error, not foo.FooError.

key: General|Python|Exceptions|Catch-all only to re-raise or isolate
claim: Never catch Exception or use bare except unless you are re-raising, or building a deliberate isolation point (like a thread's outermost guard) that RECORDS what it suppresses — catch-alls also swallow misspelled names, sys.exit, and Ctrl-C.

Avoid mutable module-level or class-attribute state — it breaks encapsulation (two databases at once becomes impossible) and runs at import time; if truly needed, underscore-prefix it, gate access through functions, and document why.
Module-level CONSTANTS in UPPER_SNAKE_CASE are encouraged — _MAX_RETRY_COUNT for internal, PUBLIC_DEFAULT for API surface; constants are the acceptable face of module-level state.

key: General|Python|Functions|Nest only to close over a value
claim: Define nested functions only when closing over a local value; never nest just to hide a helper — underscore-prefix it at module level instead so tests can still reach it.
Comprehensions may not have multiple for clauses or stacked filters — '[(x, y) for x in range(10) for y in range(5) if x*y > 10]' becomes an explicit loop; optimize for readability, not conciseness.

key: General|Python|Iteration|Default iterators no keys no readlines
claim: Iterate containers directly — 'for key in adict', 'for line in afile' — never adict.keys() or afile.readlines(); and never mutate a container while iterating it.
Generators are fine: document them with 'Yields:' instead of 'Returns:', and when one manages an expensive resource, wrap it so cleanup is forced rather than left to garbage collection.

key: General|Python|Functions|Lambdas one line operator module first
claim: Lambdas are for one-liners; past ~60-80 characters use a named nested function, and for trivial operations prefer the operator module — operator.mul over 'lambda x, y: x * y'.
Conditional expressions are fine when each portion fits on one line ('yes' if predicate(value) else 'no'); the moment it needs awkward breaking, write a full if statement.

Default argument values evaluate ONCE at module load — so no mutable defaults, and no calls like time.time() or flag reads in the def line; default to None (or an immutable tuple) and construct inside.

Properties are for trivial, cheap, unsurprising computation only — a property that merely passes through get/set is banned (make the attribute public), and don't use properties for behavior subclasses may need to override.

key: General|Python|Idioms|x or default hides falsy values
claim: Don't write 'x = x or []' to apply defaults — it silently replaces every falsy value, not just None; write 'if x is None: x = []'.

For integers, prefer explicit comparison ('if i % 10 == 0') over implicit truthiness — implicit false risks treating None as 0; also remember '0' the string is truthy, and numpy arrays need .size for emptiness.

A closure sees its enclosing variable's CURRENT value, not the value at definition — a function closing over a loop variable reads the final iteration's value; bind it as a default argument if you need the snapshot.

key: General|Python|Decorators|Decorators run at import write tests
claim: Use decorators only with clear advantage: they execute at import time, so no external dependencies (files, sockets, connections) inside them, failures there are nearly unrecoverable, and they deserve their own unit tests.
Never use @staticmethod (write a module-level function) unless an existing API forces it; use @classmethod only for named constructors or routines touching process-wide state like a cache.

key: General|Python|Concurrency|Builtin atomicity is not a contract
claim: Do not rely on the atomicity of built-in types or variable assignment across threads — use queue.Queue to communicate between threads, and threading.Condition over raw low-level locks.

Avoid metaclasses, bytecode access, dynamic inheritance, import hacks, __del__ cleanup, and reflection tricks — compactness now costs comprehension forever; stdlib users of these internals (dataclasses, enum, abc) remain fine to use.

key: General|Python|Typing|Annotate public APIs check at build
claim: Annotate public APIs and run a type checker in the build — annotations convert a class of runtime errors into build-time errors and double as enforced documentation.
Never terminate lines with semicolons and never use them to put two statements on one line.

key: General|Python|Layout|Break at the highest syntactic level
claim: When wrapping, break lines at the highest possible syntactic level — and if a line breaks twice, break at the SAME level both times; never backslash-continue.
No parentheses around return values or conditions unless they're for line continuation or a tuple — 'return foo' not 'return (foo)', 'if not x:' not 'if not(x):'.
Use a trailing comma only when the closing bracket is on its own line — auto-formatters like Black read that trailing comma as 'one item per line', making it a layout control you choose deliberately.
Two blank lines between top-level definitions, one between methods and after a class docstring, and none immediately following a def line.
Most .py files need no shebang; only the program's entry file gets '#!/usr/bin/env python3', since the line only matters when the kernel executes the file directly.
A function docstring must let someone WRITE A CALL without reading the body: calling syntax and semantics, not implementation — and a function that mutates an argument must say so.

Document functions with Args:, Returns: (Yields: for generators), and Raises: sections; don't document exceptions raised when the caller violates the documented API — that would make misuse behavior part of the contract.

key: General|Python|Docstrings|Docstring on public nontrivial or non-obvious
claim: A docstring is mandatory for any function that is public API, nontrivially sized, or non-obvious; an overriding method may skip it only when decorated @override and the contract is unchanged.

A class docstring's first line describes what an INSTANCE represents — 'The address of a cheese shop.', never 'Class that describes…' — and exceptions describe the condition ('No more cheese is available.'), not where they're raised; public attributes get an Attributes: section.
Comment what you'd otherwise have to explain in review — and never describe the code itself: assume the reader knows Python better than you; they're missing your intent, not the language.
Format with f-strings, %, or .format() — never build messages by '+' concatenation of values; a single join of two strings is fine, formatting by addition is not.

key: General|Python|Strings|Accumulate in a list then join
claim: Never accumulate strings with += in a loop — it can go quadratic, and CPython's optimization is an implementation detail; append parts to a list and ''.join() once, or write to io.StringIO.
Multi-line string literals ignore the program's indentation; use textwrap.dedent() or concatenated single-line strings so the source stays indented without embedding stray leading spaces.

Call logging functions with a literal %-pattern as the first argument and the values as separate arguments — logging.info('Version: %s', v) — never an f-string: the unexpanded pattern stays queryable and the message renders lazily only if a handler wants it.

Error messages must precisely match the real condition, mark interpolated values unambiguously (f'Not a probability: {p=}'), and stay greppable — never claim more than you verified ('could not remove (reason: %r)', not 'already deleted').

Explicitly close files, sockets, and connection-like resources with a with statement — finalizer-based cleanup is undefined-time (GC may never run promptly) and stray references in tracebacks or globals extend lifetimes indefinitely.

key: General|Python|Errors|Unused arguments deleted with a comment
claim: Silence an unused-argument warning by 'del unused_arg  # Unused.' at the top of the function — it keeps keyword callers working and actually enforces non-use, unlike underscore-renaming.
